schools.summary: The better reading is ‘righteousness’ (covering all religious duty); ‘take heed’ (prosechete) warns against the motive of being ‘seen of men,’ which forfeits the Father’s reward (cf. 5:16 — not forbidding being seen, but doing to be seen).

Augustine: the love of human glory is felt only by him who wars against it; Gregory: seeking our own glory casts our deeds out of God’s sight; the Gloss: doing the commandments for heavenly, not earthly, wages.
Chrysostom: vainglory is ‘near akin to virtue,’ a beast hard to discern that steals upon the doer of good.
‘Hypocrite’ = stage-player acting a part; ‘sound a trumpet’ likely a vivid metaphor for public show (no literal Jewish custom documented); ‘they have their reward’ — the glory of men, paid in full.
Jerome/Augustine: the trumpeter courts applause; Gregory distinguishes those who do good openly without seeking glory; Pseudo-Chrysostom: alms, prayer, fasting the three chief virtues.

debates.question: Is ‘sound a trumpet’ literal or metaphorical?

sides.position: metaphorical — a vivid figure for ostentation
possibly literal — summoning the poor / the alms-chest trumpets

themes: glory of men the whole wage

schools.summary: A proverbial hyperbole for extremest secrecy — give so privately you scarcely know it yourself; ‘as secretly as is consistent with doing it at all’ (Wesley).
Calvin: be satisfied with God as the only witness, not carried away by vanity — the death of self-regard, forgetting one’s own gift.

‘Openly’ is likely not genuine; the reward is the Father’s, ‘which seeth in secret’ — he sees the act and every motive; a principle (private charity hidden), not an absolute rule (public work may require publicity).
Calvin: reconciled with ‘let your light shine’ (5:16) — Christ forbids not being seen but doing in order to be seen; the seeing Father is sufficient audience and sure rewarder.

themes: the Father who seeth in secret

schools.summary: The hypocrites contrive to be praying (standing, at the appointed hours) in synagogues and street-corners ‘to be seen of men’ — looking round to men while feigning to address God; ‘they have their reward.’
Augustine: not the being seen but the praying in order to be seen condemns; Cyprian: pray secretly, assured God hears everywhere; Calvin: a shameful profanation of God’s name.
The ‘closet’ (tameion) with the shut door images withdrawn, private prayer directed to God alone; the reward is the Father’s ‘which seeth in secret’ (‘openly’ doubtful, as v.4).

Cyprian: pray in withdrawn places; yet the command strikes at the motive, not the place (Christ and the apostles prayed publicly, Acts 1:14) — the surest guard against praying to be seen is to pray unseen.

‘Vain repetitions’ (battalogēsēte) = babbling, empty repetition (the stammerer, or the poet Battus); the fault is not length but meaningless words, the heathen hoping to be ‘heard for their much speaking.’
Augustine/Gloss: the Gentiles think much speaking informs and wins their gods; Gregory: true prayer is the groans of repentance, not set forms; Chrysostom: nor asking improper things.

themes: God not worn by many words

schools.summary: Prayer is not to inform God (who knows our needs before we ask, Lk 12:30) but to humble us, stir desire, and exercise faith; the remedy against superstitious wordiness.
Calvin: the Father’s foreknowledge is the sufficient cure for the superstition of many words; we come to a Father, not an aloof deity to be informed and pressed.

‘After this manner’ (houtōs) sanctions both the words and the pattern (the ‘Model Prayer’); not a binding fixed form (Calvin from Luke); ‘Our Father in heaven’ — confident yet reverent; ‘hallowed’ = reverenced, glorified by us.
Cyprian/Chrysostom: ‘Father’ confesses adoption, forgiveness, brotherhood with the Only-begotten; ‘Our’ embraces all the brethren; ‘in heaven’ lifts the heart.
‘Thy kingdom come’ = that God reign everywhere and his laws be obeyed (the Messianic hope, Isa 11); ‘thy will be done… as in heaven’ governs the petitions — earth to mirror heaven’s obedience.
Cyprian: we ask to be enabled to do God’s will, done ‘in us’; Chrysostom: before we reach heaven, we pray to make earth into heaven; Jerome: the heavenly will is done without fail.

themes: ‘thy will be done… as in heaven’

schools.summary: The rare word epiousion (30+ explanations) read as ‘daily,’ ‘for the morrow,’ or ‘supersubstantial’; ‘bread’ = all needful to sustain life (Mt 4:4; Deut 8:3); a sufficiency, for the day only — daily dependence on God.
Cyprian/Cyril: the spiritual/eucharistic ‘bread of life,’ Christ himself; Gregory: ‘our’ bread, yet God’s to give.
‘Debts’ = sins (Jewish idiom; Luke ‘sins,’ 11:4), a debt to God’s justice we cannot pay; ‘as we forgive’ is not the merit but the necessary evidence — the forgiven heart forgives.
Augustine: against the Pelagians — even the righteous must daily pray ‘forgive us’; Chrysostom: for the faithful; Pseudo-Chrysostom: the unforgiving who say ‘I forgive’ pray with a lie.

themes: forgiving as the evidence of being forgiven

schools.summary: ‘Lead us not into temptation’ (God tempts none, Jas 1:13) = suffer us not to be led/overcome, a prayer of self-distrust; ‘deliver us from evil’ / ‘the evil one’; the doxology absent from the oldest MSS (most judge it added, though ancient and fitting, cf. 1 Chr 29:11).
The petition owns our weakness and casts us on God’s keeping; the doxology fitly ends the prayer with God — kingdom, power, and glory his, as it began with the Father.

debates.question: Is the closing doxology original to Matthew?

sides.position: no — absent from the oldest MSS, a liturgical addition
ancient and fitting, retained in worship

schools.summary: Christ enforces the forgiveness-clause of the prayer (v.12): God will not hear us unless we forgive; not the price but the necessary mark of received pardon (cf. Mt 18:23-35).
Chrysostom: nothing so likens us to God as forgiving an injurer; Cyprian: we shall be judged by our own sentence; Augustine: this petition singled out for enforcement.

The reverse stated without qualification: the unforgiving will not be forgiven (Eph 4:31; Jas 2:13) — not that forgiving earns pardon, but that an unforgiving spirit proves the heart never truly received it.

themes: a sign of an unrenewed heart

schools.summary: The hypocrites ‘disfigure’ their faces with a studied gloom (skythrōpoi) to ‘appear to fast’; Christ corrects the manner, not enjoining fasting itself (an established duty); ‘they have their reward’ — men’s notice.
Gregory/Leo: a fast that seeks the esteem of men, or comes of ‘the arts of deceit,’ is not pure; Chrysostom: some surpass the hypocrites in vanity.
To ‘anoint and wash’ (against the fasting custom of neglecting both) = appear as on any ordinary day, concealing the fast; not a literal command but a figure for hiding it (Augustine).
Gregory/Leo: true fasting opens the hand to alms and cuts off vices, not abstinence from food only; the anointing/washing the inward gladness of a fast unto God.

The reverse of the hypocrites: fast ‘unto thy Father which is in secret,’ not to ‘appear unto men’; the reward is the Father’s who ‘seeth in secret’ (‘openly’ not genuine, as vv.4,6) — the refrain sealing the threefold teaching on alms, prayer, fasting.

‘Treasure not up your treasures’ (present imperative, the habit of hoarding); moth, rust, and thieves cover all earthly wealth (clothes, metals, valuables) — perishing and insecure; not forbidding use but treasuring as one’s hope.

Chrysostom: vanity gone, contempt of riches follows (love of money springs from love of praise); Augustine: the heart cannot be pure while working for earthly gain; Jerome: every man serves what he is in bondage to.
To ‘lay up treasure in heaven’ = provide for eternal felicity, the good works and character that follow us (Lk 12:33), safe from moth, rust, and thief — an imperishable store.
Calvin: they lay up in heaven who, not entangled in the world’s snares, make it their care to meditate on the heavenly life; the means are faith, good works, and charity (1 Tim 6:18-19).
A self-evident law of the soul: the heart (love, thoughts, desires) follows the treasure; so where one lays up treasure determines where the heart dwells — earthbound or heavenly.

themes: ‘where your treasure is, there your heart’
the captivity or freedom of the heart

schools.summary: As the eye is the body’s lamp, so the inner purpose/affection lights the whole life; a ‘single’ (sound, undivided) eye = a heart fixed on God alone, filling the life with light.
Augustine: the eye = our purpose, which if pure makes all our works good; Hilary: the light of the heart; Chrysostom: a sensible illustration of singleness toward God.

themes: ‘the light of the body is the eye’

schools.summary: The ‘evil eye’ = a corrupt, covetous, divided purpose (a Hebrew idiom for avarice); it fills the whole life with darkness; if the inner light (conscience, purpose) is itself dark, ‘how great is that darkness!’
Augustine/Gregory: a purpose overclouded with evil makes even good works darkness; the terror that the very faculty meant to give light is itself dark.
‘Mammon’ (Syriac for riches) is a rival master; a slave cannot give two masters the undivided service each demands — the love of money is incompatible with the service of God, not merely dangerous.
Augustine: to serve mammon is to serve the prince of this world; Jerome: the covetous Christian cannot serve both Christ and riches — the great either/or.
‘Take no thought’ (merimnate) forbids not foresight or labour but anxious, distrustful care; Christ argues from greater to less — God who gave life and body will give food and raiment.
Augustine: he who would not serve mammon must not be enslaved by bodily cares; the anxiety is the root and fruit of the love of money.

themes: trust the Father who gave life

schools.summary: The wild birds, neither sowing nor storing, are fed by the Father; how much more his children, ‘much better than they’ — an argument from the lesser creatures to the greater.
Augustine/Gloss: the birds’ being fed is no warrant for idleness — the lesson is trust in providence, not sloth (the birds gather, but do not fret).

‘A cubit to his stature’ (height) or ‘a span to his age’ (lifespan) — the Greek allows either; the argument is the futility of anxiety, which cannot add the least to body or days, all being in God’s hand alone.
Passing from food to raiment, Christ bids us ‘consider the lilies,’ which toil not nor spin yet are clothed by God; not to be over-allegorised (Augustine) — plain examples of providence.
Jerome: no royal purple or silk can vie with the flowers; the Gloss: God’s providence in what is beyond human industry — if God so clothes the lilies, how much more his children.
The emphatic ‘I say unto you’: the wild flower outshines Solomon at his most magnificent (1 Kgs 10:4-7); God’s clothing of the lilies exceeds all human wealth and art can achieve.

themes: ‘even Solomon… not arrayed like one of these’

schools.summary: Lesser-to-greater: God clothes the perishing ‘grass of the field’ (dried for the oven) though it lasts a day — how much more his children; ‘O ye of little faith’ (oligopistoi, a Talmudic phrase) gently rebukes anxiety as a failure of faith.
The command repeated for emphasis: Christ forbids not seeking food, drink, raiment but the anxious ‘saying, What shall we eat?’ — the fretful, doubting care, summing up all the foregoing arguments.
Anxious seeking marks the Gentiles, who know no fatherly care (a tone of pity); the remedy is that ‘your heavenly Father knoweth’ your needs — Chrysostom: ‘Father,’ not ‘God,’ to raise their hope.
Augustine: God foreknew our needs before all time; to fret is to forget our adoption and act as if fatherless.

‘Seek first the kingdom of God and his righteousness’ — God’s reign and the holiness he requires, before all earthly concern; ‘all these things shall be added’ as a free addition (1 Tim 4:8; Ps 37:25); Jesus centres the kingdom, not mere ethics.
Calvin: anxious care betrays a gross neglect of the soul; the remedy for worry is to seek first the kingdom, and the lesser things fall into place.

themes: holy ambition the cure for care

schools.summary: Anxiety for the future forbidden as ‘fruitless care… of distrust’; each day brings its own concerns, met in their season; ‘sufficient unto the day is the evil thereof’ — live one day at a time.
Chrysostom: nothing pains the spirit like anxiety, and worry for the morrow needlessly multiplies the day’s burden; the future left in God’s hand.
